Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation Chapter Map

Chapter-level reading map for Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation.

Source

This map organises the generated chapter notes for Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation (Newcomer, Hatry, and Wholey 2015).

Reading Route

  • Plan useful evaluations: Chapters 1-12 cover design, stakeholders, logic, exploratory work, monitoring, causal designs, case studies, multisite work, community change, and culturally responsive evaluation.
  • Collect defensible data: Chapters 13-21 cover records, surveys, role playing, trained observation, fieldwork, internet methods, interviews, focus groups, and stories.
  • Analyze transparently: Chapters 22-25 cover qualitative analysis, statistics, economic analysis, and evidence synthesis.
  • Support use: Chapters 26-31 cover pitfalls, recommendations, reports, contracting, politics, quality, standards, and evaluation capacity.

Chapters

Chapter Source Pages Main Design Question Link
1. Planning and Designing Useful Evaluations 49-77 Useful evaluation begins by matching information needs, contextual constraints, rigor, and communication to the decisions the evaluation must inform. chapter-01-planning-and-designing-useful-evaluations.qmd
2. Analyzing and Engaging Stakeholders 78-103 Stakeholder analysis should precede and guide engagement so evaluations have clear users, purposes, roles, and adaptive strategies for changing settings. chapter-02-analyzing-and-engaging-stakeholders.qmd
3. Using Logic Models 104-129 Logic models help evaluators and program staff clarify program theory, identify measures, and frame performance stories without treating the model as final truth. chapter-03-using-logic-models.qmd
4. Exploratory Evaluation 130-149 Exploratory evaluation helps programs clarify goals, feasibility, data, measures, and next evaluation steps before committing to a definitive study. chapter-04-exploratory-evaluation.qmd
5. Performance Measurement 150-178 Performance measurement systems can support improvement when measures are valid, understandable, affordable, and presented for decisions rather than compliance alone. chapter-05-performance-measurement.qmd
6. Comparison Group Designs 179-201 Comparison group designs estimate program effects when randomization is unavailable, but their credibility depends on how well they approximate the counterfactual. chapter-06-comparison-group-designs.qmd
7. Randomized Controlled Trials 202-224 Randomized controlled trials can provide strong causal evidence when assignment, implementation, measurement, ethics, and analysis remain aligned. chapter-07-randomized-controlled-trials.qmd
8. Conducting Case Studies 225-248 Case studies integrate multiple sources to understand programs in context, including exploratory, descriptive, and explanatory evaluation questions. chapter-08-conducting-case-studies.qmd
9. Recruitment and Retention of Study Participants 249-267 Recruitment and retention are design-quality issues because sample coverage and continued participation determine whether evaluation findings are credible. chapter-09-recruitment-and-retention-of-study-participants.qmd
10. Designing, Managing, and Analyzing Multisite Evaluations 268-299 Multisite evaluations require common design discipline while preserving enough site-level detail to explain variation across contexts. chapter-10-designing-managing-and-analyzing-multisite-evaluations.qmd
11. Evaluating Community Change Programs 300-322 Community change evaluation must handle broad aims, place-based complexity, shifting implementation, difficult units of analysis, and weak causal leverage. chapter-11-evaluating-community-change-programs.qmd
12. Culturally Responsive Evaluation 323-357 Culturally responsive evaluation strengthens validity, rigor, and responsibility by making culture and context integral to every evaluation stage. chapter-12-culturally-responsive-evaluation.qmd
13. Using Agency Records 367-384 Agency records can be efficient evaluation data, but only after checking definitions, completeness, access, consistency, and fit with evaluation questions. chapter-13-using-agency-records.qmd
14. Using Surveys 385-424 Surveys can provide comparable evidence from clients, households, organizations, or officials when sampling, question wording, mode, and response quality are managed well. chapter-14-using-surveys.qmd
15. Role Playing 425-453 Role playing can reveal service quality and differential treatment by observing standardized transactions that other methods may miss. chapter-15-role-playing.qmd
16. Using Ratings by Trained Observers 454-487 Trained observer ratings turn observed conditions into systematic data when definitions, rating anchors, sampling, training, and supervision are explicit. chapter-16-using-ratings-by-trained-observers.qmd
17. Collecting Data in the Field 488-518 Field data collection makes evaluation evidence more contextual and credible when site visits, interviews, observations, and records are organized systematically. chapter-17-collecting-data-in-the-field.qmd
18. Using the Internet 519-537 Internet tools can support evaluation data collection and communication, but they require attention to coverage, access, privacy, usability, and data integrity. chapter-18-using-the-internet.qmd
19. Semi-Structured Interviews 538-551 Semi-structured interviews help evaluators learn from knowledgeable respondents while preserving enough consistency for analysis. chapter-19-semi-structured-interviews.qmd
20. Focus Group Interviewing 552-576 Focus groups generate interactional evidence that can inform design, interpretation, and understanding of program experiences. chapter-20-focus-group-interviewing.qmd
21. Using Stories in Evaluation 577-597 Stories can deepen evaluation understanding and communication when collected and analyzed with qualitative discipline. chapter-21-using-stories-in-evaluation.qmd
22. Analyzing Qualitative Data 603-635 Qualitative analysis turns observations, documents, and interviews into credible findings through coding, display, interpretation, triangulation, and verification. chapter-22-analyzing-qualitative-data.qmd
23. Using Statistics in Evaluation 636-677 Statistical analysis strengthens evaluation conclusions when techniques match questions, samples, measurement scales, assumptions, and reporting needs. chapter-23-using-statistics-in-evaluation.qmd
24. Cost-Effectiveness and Cost-Benefit Analysis 678-714 Economic analysis helps decision makers compare value by relating program costs to effects or monetized benefits. chapter-24-cost-effectiveness-and-cost-benefit-analysis.qmd
25. Meta-Analyses, Systematic Reviews, and Evaluation Syntheses 715-740 Evidence synthesis helps evaluators learn from prior studies by making search, inclusion, quality assessment, and synthesis procedures explicit. chapter-25-meta-analyses-systematic-reviews-and-evaluation-syntheses.qmd
26. Pitfalls in Evaluations 745-776 Recognizing evaluation pitfalls strengthens credibility because every evaluation has limitations that should be anticipated, reduced, and explained. chapter-26-pitfalls-in-evaluations.qmd
27. Writing Recommendations 777-790 Recommendations are useful when they flow from findings, respect decision contexts, and offer practical options for improving programs. chapter-27-writing-recommendations.qmd
28. Writing Evaluation Reports 791-806 Evaluation reports should convey findings, methods, limits, and implications clearly enough to stimulate constructive action. chapter-28-writing-evaluation-reports.qmd
29. Contracting for Evaluation Products and Services 807-840 Evaluation contracting requires clear plans, realistic requests, qualified contractors, active monitoring, and product-quality management. chapter-29-contracting-for-evaluation-products-and-services.qmd
30. Evaluation in Government and the Politics of Evaluation 841-857 Government evaluation use depends on navigating political and bureaucratic realities while protecting credibility and improvement purposes. chapter-30-evaluation-in-government-and-the-politics-of-evaluation.qmd
31. Evaluation Challenges, Issues, and Trends 858-874 The final chapter frames evaluation quality, evaluator capacity, standards, ethics, use, and future trends as continuing responsibilities. chapter-31-evaluation-challenges-issues-and-trends.qmd

Legacy Extracts

  • Evaluation Planning and Design
  • Planning and Designing Useful Evaluations
  • Performance Measurement and Program Evaluation

References

Newcomer, Kathryn E., Harry P. Hatry, and Joseph S. Wholey. 2015. Handbook of Practical Program Evaluation. Jossey-Bass.
